2: Mount Hermon (2,814 m, the highest point in the region) is the most likely site of the Transfiguration, consistent with the Caesarea Philippi location of the preceding passage and the six-day interval (9:2). The phrase they saw no one any more but Jesus only (v.8) — after Moses and Elijah depart — is the key theological statement: Moses (Torah) and Elijah (Prophets) were temporary presences who appeared to attend and then departed; Jesus alone remains. The entire OT revelation is subordinated to and surpassed by the one person whose presence is permanent and defining.
1: The saying that some standing there “will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God come with power” stands immediately before the Transfiguration (six days later, 9:2), and Mark’s arrangement suggests the Transfiguration is the initial fulfillment — a proleptic glimpse of the glorified Son of Man. The idiom “taste death” (geusetai thanatou) appears in Jewish apocalyptic texts (4 Ezra 6:26; cf. Ps 89:48) for undergoing death as an experience. The phrase “come in power” uses royal-enthronement vocabulary drawn from the Psalms and Daniel 7.
3: White garments in Jewish apocalyptic literature signal divine or angelic glory: the Ancient of Days wears white clothing (Dan 7:9); the heavenly beings in 1 Enoch and 2 Baruch are clothed in white light. The comparison “whiter than anyone in the world could bleach them” grounds the supernatural in a practical benchmark — professional cloth-bleachers using sunlight, sulfur, or urine were a recognizable urban trade in the Roman world. The point is that no human process could produce what the disciples see.
4: Moses and Elijah together represent the Torah and the Prophets — the two great divisions of Hebrew scripture in first-century Jewish usage (cf. Matt 5:17; Luke 16:16). Both figures had mysterious departures from the world: Moses’s burial site was hidden (Deut 34:5-6) and later tradition disputed whether he died at all (Jude 9; Josephus Ant. 4.326); Elijah was taken to heaven without dying (2 Kgs 2:11). Both were associated with Israel’s definitive moments at Sinai/Horeb. Jewish tradition expected both figures to reappear in the final age: Moses as the prophet like Moses of Deut 18:15, Elijah as the forerunner of Mal 4:5-6.
5: Peter’s proposal to make three shelters (skenai) may reflect the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot) framework. Sukkot required Jewish families to construct temporary booths for seven days to commemorate the wilderness period (Lev 23:42-43); Zech 14:16-19 anticipated a universal Sukkot in the messianic age when all nations would come to Jerusalem to celebrate it. If Peter responds to what looks like the eschatological arrival of the kingdom, making booths for Moses, Elijah, and Jesus would be a fitting Sukkot response. The address “Rabbi” is the Aramaic teacher-title that Mark’s Gentile audience would have recognized from synagogue usage.
6: Mark’s candid portrayal records that Peter “did not know what to say, they were so frightened.” The Greek ekphoboi gar egenonto is strong language for terror — the same root used in the disciples’ response to the stilled storm (4:41). The admission of speechlessness in the face of overwhelming divine encounter is a feature of Hebrew theophany narratives: Moses hides his face at the burning bush (Exod 3:6), Isaiah confesses he is undone (Isa 6:5), Ezekiel falls on his face (Ezek 1:28). Peter speaks anyway from overwhelm rather than wisdom, and Mark is frank about this.
7: The overshadowing cloud (nephelē episkiazousa) echoes the Shekinah-glory cloud of the Exodus: it guided Israel in the wilderness (Exod 13:21-22), covered Sinai when God spoke to Moses (Exod 24:15-18), and filled the completed tabernacle and temple (Exod 40:34-38; 1 Kgs 8:10-11). The divine voice identifies Jesus using language drawn from Ps 2:7 (“You are my Son”) and Isa 42:1 (the Servant “in whom my soul delights”). The command “listen to him” (akouete autou) quotes Deut 18:15, where Moses promises a prophet like himself to whom Israel must listen — the cloud-voice identifies Jesus as the fulfillment of that promise.
8: The disappearance of Moses and Elijah, leaving the disciples seeing “only Jesus,” is the narrative resolution of the Transfiguration. The representatives of Torah and Prophets appeared as witnesses and then withdrew; Jesus alone remains as the permanent, defining presence. Moses and Elijah could not remain because their role was preparatory. The scene enacts what the divine voice commanded: the disciples are left with Jesus alone as the one to hear. This spatial resolution is the theological point.
9: The secrecy order at the descent is tied to a specific time limit: “until the Son of Man had risen from the dead.” This is the first time the messianic-secret commands are explicitly linked to the resurrection as the moment when disclosure becomes possible. In Mark’s broader pattern, Jesus suppresses premature messianic identification because the disciples and crowds hold expectations — military deliverer, immediate kingdom establishment — that would distort the announcement before the cross redefines what Messiahship means.
10: The disciples debate the meaning of “rising from the dead” among themselves. Standard Pharisaic theology held to a general bodily resurrection at the end of the age (Dan 12:2; 2 Macc 7). The Sadducees denied resurrection altogether. Neither group had a framework for one individual rising from the dead before the general resurrection — this was a conceptual novelty. Their confusion is not ignorance of resurrection in general but puzzlement about what a particular individual’s rising in advance of the general resurrection could mean.
11: The disciples’ question about Elijah reflects a standard item of Second Temple eschatological expectation: Mal 4:5-6 promises that Elijah will come “before the great and dreadful day of the LORD,” to turn the hearts of the fathers to their children. Sir 48:10 (second century BCE) repeats the expectation. The scribes taught that Elijah’s restoration of all things must precede the Messiah’s arrival. If Jesus is acting as Messiah, but Elijah has not yet come — or has he? — the scribal expectation creates the logical problem the disciples are working through.
12: Jesus affirms the Elijah-comes-first tradition and then poses a counter-question: scripture also indicates that the Son of Man must suffer greatly and be rejected. This connects two prophetic streams — the expected forerunner and the suffering of the Son of Man — and implies that the rejection of the forerunner (the Elijah-figure) and the rejection of the Son of Man follow the same pattern. The texts may include the Servant Songs (Isa 53), the rejected stone (Ps 118:22), and Ps 22. The logic is: if the forerunner was not received in glory but in rejection, the Son of Man should expect the same.
13: The implicit identification of John the Baptist as Elijah is clearer here than anywhere else in Mark (Matthew makes it explicit at 17:12-13). John wore Elijah’s characteristic clothing (Mark 1:6; cf. 2 Kgs 1:8) and conducted a ministry of preparation. “They have done to him everything they wished” echoes Jezebel’s persecution of Elijah (1 Kgs 19:2,10). Herodias plays the Jezebel role; Herod Antipas plays the compromised Ahab. The pattern of prophet-sending and prophet-rejection runs as a continuous thread through the Hebrew Bible (cf. Neh 9:26; Matt 23:37).
14: The descent from the mountain to the scene of the disciples’ failure carries a structural echo of Moses’s descent from Sinai to find Israel worshipping the golden calf (Exod 32) — from divine glory to human faithlessness in the valley below. Mark deploys the typology lightly: the disciples’ failure in exorcism is not idolatry, but the contrast between mountaintop encounter and valley-level unbelief has the same shape. The scribes arguing with the disciples continue the pattern of official opposition that has characterized the Galilean section of the Gospel (2:6, 3:22, 7:1).
15: The crowd’s overwhelming amazement (exethambēthēsan) at seeing Jesus may carry a residual trace of the Transfiguration’s glory — ancient interpreters including Origen read it this way. The verb root (thambos) is the same used at 1:27, 10:24, and 10:32 for responses to Jesus that exceed ordinary astonishment. Whether a lingering radiance or simply the impact of Jesus’s arrival on a scene of public failure, Mark places their amazed welcome in deliberate contrast with the disciples’ inability.
16: Jesus enters the dispute between the scribes and disciples by addressing the scribes directly. In Mark’s pattern, scribal challenges are consistently met with direct counter-engagement rather than evasion (2:8; 3:23; 7:6; 12:35). The question “What are you arguing with them about?” does not wait for the disciples to answer; it establishes Jesus as the one who sets the terms of the confrontation.
17: The symptoms described — inability to speak, violent seizure, foaming at the mouth, gnashing of teeth, rigidity — correspond to what modern medicine classifies as tonic-clonic (grand mal) epilepsy. First-century medicine lacked a neurological framework: Hippocrates argued in “On the Sacred Disease” that epilepsy had natural causes, but his was a minority view; the dominant framework, shared across Jewish and Gentile cultures, attributed such symptoms to spirit possession. The father’s clinical description conveys years of helpless observation.
18: The disciples’ failed exorcism attempt is reported without accusation but with devastating effect. They had been given authority over unclean spirits (6:7) and had successfully exercised it during the mission journey (6:13), yet here they failed. The contrast sets up both the rebuke to come (v.19) and the private teaching in vv.28-29. The word “rigid” (xērainō) indicates the postictal state of muscular exhaustion following a grand mal episode — a clinically precise detail consistent with eyewitness tradition.
19: “You unbelieving generation” (genea apistos) echoes the prophetic lament formula. Moses used it at Num 14:27 and Deut 32:5,20 to address Israel’s persistent covenant-refusal; Isaiah deployed it at Isa 65:2 (“a rebellious people”). In prophetic usage, “generation” (genea) describes not an age group but a people constituted by a characteristic pattern of behavior — here, faithlessness despite repeated demonstrations of divine power. The double question “how long shall I stay with you? how long shall I put up with you?” is divine exasperation at covenant unfaithfulness, not a statement of permanent abandonment.
20: The spirit’s violent reaction to Jesus’s presence — before any command is spoken — follows the pattern of 1:26 and 5:7: the unclean spirits consistently recognize and react to Jesus before he acts. The convulsion at the point of encounter is the spirit’s response to proximity to divine authority, not random symptomatic recurrence. The crowd’s presence makes the confrontation public and increases the stakes of what follows.
21: Jesus asks the father a diagnostic question: “How long has he been like this?” This is pastoral attentiveness as much as information-gathering. The father’s answer — “from childhood” — communicates not just chronology but years of suffering, failed remedies, and depleted hope. The detail about fire and water (v.22) reveals that the boy’s condition has been life-threatening on multiple occasions. Jesus draws out the full extent of the suffering before acting, giving voice to what the father has endured.
22: The father’s plea “if you can do anything” (ei ti dynasai) is hedged by the disciples’ failure — his hope has been depleted by what he has already witnessed. “Take pity on us” invokes the language of compassion (splanchnistheis) that Mark uses when Jesus healed the leper (1:41) and saw the crowd as sheep without a shepherd (6:34). The inclusive “us” indicates the father’s own suffering is bound up with his son’s; the plea is for the whole household.
23: Jesus echoes the father’s conditional back at him: “The ‘if you can’” — the repetition is pointed, redirecting the question of capacity from Jesus to the father’s orientation. By saying “everything is possible for the one who believes,” Jesus is not making healing conditional on sufficient human performance but calling the father to reorient from the disciples’ failure to the authority now standing before him. The statement opens the possibility of healing by clarifying where the father must direct his trust.
24: The father’s cry “I believe; help me overcome my unbelief” holds faith and its absence simultaneously — one of the most honest prayers in the NT. The Psalms of lament (Ps 22, 88, 102) demonstrate that approaching God from within genuine crisis, without performed confidence, is a deep biblical tradition. The father does not manufacture faith he does not have; he presents his actual state and asks for grace to supply what he lacks. This prayer has become a touchstone in Christian devotional practice precisely because it refuses the pretense that faith and doubt cannot coexist in the same moment of prayer.
25: Jesus acts quickly when he sees another crowd running to the scene, preventing the healing from becoming a public spectacle. The command is more elaborate than earlier exorcisms in Mark: the spirit is named specifically (“deaf and mute spirit”), commanded to come out, and prohibited from returning permanently. Naming a spirit and issuing a prohibition against re-entry reflect features of Jewish exorcistic practice where persistent resistance required more explicit command (Josephus Ant. 8.46-47 describes Solomon’s exorcistic technique involving invocation, prohibition, and a test of success).
26: The spirit’s violent exit — shrieking, convulsing the boy — leaves the child appearing dead. The crowd’s reaction “He is dead” recalls the ambiguity at Jairus’s daughter (5:39: “the child is not dead but asleep”). The threshold between severe illness, apparent death, and death itself was porous in ancient medical understanding. The scene stages a crisis that the next verse resolves in language that echoes resurrection.
27: Jesus takes the boy by the hand and lifts him up using ēgeiren — the standard resurrection verb in the NT (used in Mark 16:6 at the empty tomb, in 1 Cor 15 for Christ’s resurrection, and throughout the Gospel tradition for healing-raisings). The identical gesture appears at the raising of Jairus’s daughter (5:41-42). Whether or not Mark intends a resurrection type, the verbal and gestural echoes accumulate throughout the Gospel and prepare the reader for the passion-resurrection predictions that structure this section.
28: The pattern of private indoor instruction after public ministry is a Markan structural feature (7:17; 10:10; 13:3). The house in Capernaum appears to be Peter’s house (1:29). The disciples’ question “Why couldn’t we drive it out?” assumes they should have been able to: they had been given authority over unclean spirits (6:7) and used it effectively on the mission journey (6:13). Their failure is not a failure of delegated authority in principle but a failure of how they were relying on it.
29: “This kind can come out only by prayer” implies different categories or degrees of spiritual resistance. In Jewish exorcistic tradition, particularly persistent spirits required specialized approaches: Josephus describes Solomon’s method involving prayer and specific incantation (Ant. 8.45-49); Tobit 8:1-3 uses fumigation alongside prayer against a persistent demon. The disciples had relied on delegated authority without continued prayerful dependence on its source. The variant reading “and fasting” is absent from the earliest manuscripts (P45, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and was likely a scribal addition reflecting early Christian ascetic practice.
30: The journey through Galilee in secrecy contrasts with the earlier Galilean ministry where Jesus’s fame spread everywhere (1:28, 45; 7:36). The stated reason is private instruction: the journey section (8:27-10:52) is structured around three passion predictions (8:31; 9:31; 10:33-34) as the backbone of a sustained course of teaching on suffering, service, and the nature of the kingdom. The withdrawal from public activity creates space for the inner-circle instruction the crowds cannot yet receive.
31: The second passion prediction is more specific than the first (8:31): “The Son of Man is going to be delivered into human hands.” The passive paradidotai (will be delivered/handed over) echoes the LXX of Isa 53:6 and 53:12, where the Servant is “handed over” (paredothē) to bear the iniquities of many. The irony is compressed: the Son of Man — the heavenly figure of Dan 7:13 who receives from the Ancient of Days all dominion, glory, and sovereign power — will be handed over into the hands of the very people he is appointed to rule.
32: Mark’s emphasis on the disciples’ failure to understand the passion predictions is a sustained motif throughout the journey section (8:32-33; 9:32; 10:35-40). Each prediction is followed by a display of misunderstanding: Peter rebukes Jesus (8:32), the disciples debate greatness (9:34), James and John request thrones (10:37). “They were afraid to ask him” suggests they sensed the predictions were important but could not integrate them into their expectations and feared where pressing the question would lead.
33: Capernaum served as Jesus’s operational base in Galilee throughout the early ministry (1:21; 2:1), centered in Peter’s house (1:29-34; 2:1-2). The oikos (house) in Galilean village culture was a multi-generational family compound, not a private dwelling in the modern sense; hospitality obligations meant it attracted visitors continuously. Arriving at the familiar house framed the confrontation that follows within the community’s own domestic space — an intra-group reckoning rather than a public dispute.
34: The disciples’ silence when Jesus asks about the road-argument exposes guilt, not ignorance. In an honor-shame culture, positional rank was a constant concern: the protos (first) in any group commanded the most honor, received the best treatment, and set the terms for others. Dispute over who would rank highest in the coming kingdom was entirely natural within the Mediterranean honor-competition framework — and entirely incompatible with the passion prediction they had just received. The silence (esiōpōn) is self-condemnation: the disciples recognize that what they were arguing about cannot be defended in light of what Jesus just said.
35: The seated posture of the authoritative teacher (as Moses sat at the tent door, as scribes sat to read Torah) frames what follows as a formal ruling, not casual advice. Jesus calls the Twelve to attention and inverts the social hierarchy: the protos (first, the patron) must become the eschatos (last) and the diakonos (servant, table-attendant) of all. In Greek social usage diakonos described the lowest grade of household staff — the one who waited at table, not the one seated at it. Jesus makes this figure, not the patron, the paradigm for leadership in the community he is forming.
36: In the Greco-Roman world, children had minimal social standing and no legal autonomy; they were dependents with no capacity to honor a patron in return, no social capital to offer, no position in any hierarchy. The child (paidion) represented the bottom of the social ladder. Jesus places the child physically in the center of the Twelve and takes it in his arms — enacting in a gesture the reversal he has just described in principle. The one with no social value is placed at the center of the community that has been arguing about who among them holds the most.
37: Jewish hospitality ethics required welcoming the stranger, the poor, and the traveling teacher as a sacred obligation — Abraham welcoming the three visitors (Gen 18) was the paradigmatic text. Welcoming someone “in my name” reframes the act: it is not social calculation about reciprocal honor but reception of the one Jesus is identified with. Since the child represents the honorless, the logic of the saying is that genuine welcome of the insignificant constitutes welcome of Jesus himself — and of the one who sent him. Honor-competition is structurally incompatible with this kind of welcoming.
38: John’s report that they stopped an outsider casting out demons in Jesus’s name follows immediately after the child-welcoming teaching — the juxtaposition is pointed. Mark preserves the telling slip: they stopped him because he was not following “us,” not “you.” Group identity rather than Jesus’s mission has become the boundary. In Jewish practice, invoking the name of a holy man in exorcism was recognized as legitimate (Josephus Ant. 8.46-47 on Solomon’s name); using Jesus’s name effectively signaled real alignment with his authority regardless of formal enrollment.
39: Jesus refuses to enforce organizational exclusivity when the work is genuinely effective. The logic is practical: no one who performs a powerful work in Jesus’s name will immediately turn to slander him. Effectiveness in the name is itself a kind of alignment with the one whose name is invoked. The disciples’ concern for boundary-maintenance belongs to the same honor-competition logic Jesus has been addressing since v.33 — protecting the group’s exclusive franchise rather than advancing the kingdom.
40: “Whoever is not against us is for us” is the de facto alignment principle: the outside exorcist is not actively opposed, and his use of Jesus’s name places him functionally on the side of the kingdom’s work. The saying appears inverted in Matt 12:30 (“whoever is not with me is against me”) — both are contextually appropriate: Matthew addresses hostile neutrality toward Jesus, where non-alignment equals opposition; Mark addresses sympathetic independence, where effective use of Jesus’s name constitutes de facto belonging.
41: A cup of water for a traveler was a real act of generosity in first-century Palestine, where water was carried from wells or cisterns. The Jewish ethic of gemilut hasadim (acts of loving-kindness) placed hospitality to travelers among the foundational community obligations: the Mishnah names such acts alongside Torah study and temple service as pillars on which the world stands (m. Avot 1:2). Jesus places eschatological weight on the smallest such act done in his name — reversing the honor-culture calculus that treats small, unreciprocated gifts as socially negligible.
42: The mylos onikos (donkey millstone) was the large upper stone of a commercial grain mill, pulled around the lower stone by a donkey — far heavier than the small hand-mills used in households. As a weight around the neck it would ensure certain drowning. Jewish law treated causing the death of an innocent person as the gravest category of offense; causing a vulnerable believer to fall away from faith is placed in the same register. The “little ones who believe in me” (mikroi) are vulnerable disciples rather than specifically children, though the child of vv.36-37 provides the controlling image.
43: The hyperbolic saying about cutting off the hand follows the Hebrew wisdom form of the “better-this-than-that” comparison (tob...min), common in Proverbs and Ecclesiastes, and the Mishnaic principle that one may sacrifice a limb to save the whole body. Physical mutilation was not the point and was not practiced — the saying is a rhetorical intensification making absolute the priority of kingdom-entry over any attachment that leads to Gehenna. “Gehenna” (geenna) is the Greek form of gê hinnôm — the Valley of Ben-Hinnom, a ravine running southwest of Jerusalem, below the hill of Zion.
44: This verse repeats the Isa 66:24 refrain from v.48 (“where their worm does not die and the fire is not quenched”) and is absent from the earliest manuscripts (P45, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus). It was most likely added by scribes to create formal symmetry across vv.43-48 by attaching the refrain to each of the three body-part sayings. The theological content derives from Isa 66:24; see the commentary on v.48.
45: The foot as the instrument of sinful movement — taking a person where they should not go — applies the better-than-mutilation framework of v.43 to a different instrument of action. The Gehenna warning repeats. The Valley of Ben-Hinnom in the Second Temple period was Jerusalem’s refuse site, where fires burned to consume garbage and animal carcasses. Its earlier associations with child sacrifice under Ahaz and Manasseh (2 Kgs 16:3; 2 Chr 28:3; Jer 7:31) charged the location; Jeremiah proclaimed it would become the “Valley of Slaughter” (Jer 7:32; 19:6). By the first century, geenna had become standard Jewish vocabulary for the place of final judgment (2 Bar 59:11; 4 Ezra 7:36).
46: As with v.44, this verse repeats the Isa 66:24 refrain and is absent from the earliest witnesses. It completes the scribal harmonization of the three body-part sayings by attaching the worm-and-fire formula to each. The earliest form of the passage included the refrain only once, at v.48, as the closing punctuation for the whole series. See v.48 for the Isaiah source and its original context.
47: The eye as the instrument of covetous desire appears throughout Jewish wisdom literature: Prov 4:25 warns against a wandering eye; Sir 14:8-9 describes the miser’s eye as insatiable; 1 John 2:16 later names “the lust of the eyes” among the characteristic attachments of this age. The triad of hand (action), foot (movement), eye (desire) addresses the three primary instruments of sin in a comprehensive, escalating sequence that concludes by naming the alternative destinations: the kingdom of God or Gehenna.
48: The formula “where their worm does not die and the fire is not quenched” quotes Isa 66:24 — the final verse of Isaiah’s closing vision of the new creation (Isa 66:18-24). In that vision, the restored community goes out to look at the corpses of those who rebelled against God, consumed by perpetual worm and unquenched fire. Jesus cites this as the last word of Isaiah’s canon: the prophet who opens with Jerusalem’s judgment (Isa 1:2-9) closes with the same solemn image. Citing Isaiah’s final verse as the description of Gehenna draws on the full weight of the prophetic corpus.
49: “Everyone will be salted with fire” is among the most debated sayings in Mark, but its background lies in the Levitical requirement that salt accompany every sacrificial offering: “Season all your grain offerings with salt; do not leave the salt of the covenant of your God out of your grain offerings” (Lev 2:13). The Mishnah confirms this extended to all sacrifices (m. Men. 5:3). Salt was a preservative, a purifier, and the substance of covenant ratification (Num 18:19 — “a covenant of salt before the LORD”). Combined with fire, the image suggests purifying, covenant-constituting testing — the eschatological trial through which the community is refined rather than destroyed.
50: Salt was indispensable in the ancient world: it preserved food across seasons, seasoned meals, ratified covenants, and was required on every temple sacrifice. Roman soldiers received part of their pay as salt (sal), the origin of the Latin salarium and English “salary.” Ancient salt from the Dead Sea region was often mixed with gypsum or other impurities that could render it tasteless without removing the material — the salt that “loses its saltiness” was a recognizable phenomenon. “Have salt among yourselves” — maintain the covenant integrity, preserving honesty, and purifying quality that seasons community life — “and be at peace with each other” lands the chapter’s unified teaching (greatness dispute, child-welcoming, outsider-inclusion) in a community ethic.
